summary: Ronacher dissects his April Fools microframework prank "denied" and the unexpectedly enthusiastic reception it got. He explains that the joke was built by bundling existing tools and deliberately shipping bad design choices inspired by real microframeworks. The outsized attention taught him that marketing, GitHub presence, and tiny code snippets drive adoption more than honest engineering. He argues that software complexity is often earned, citing how minimalist frameworks inevitably grow and shed naive ideas. He concludes by keeping the joke as a joke, adding better examples to his real projects, and encouraging sensible dependencies for future microframeworks.
key_points: The prank framework was a bundle of real components plus intentionally bad design choices, yet many people treated it as serious.
Bold marketing, GitHub visibility, and tiny examples attract users more than quality or honesty.
Minimal, single-file frameworks are appealing but tend to grow because real problems demand complexity.
Early "clever" design choices in microframeworks often get reversed as projects mature.
He plans to add small examples to Werkzeug/Jinja2 and keep the prank as a prank.
money_quotes: It does not matter how good intended or well written a project is, the bold marketing is king.
There is no such thing as a "bad endorsement".
There is a reason why things are as complex as they are and not easier.
However things are not that easy in reality.
Many of the initial design decisions that were plain wrong were reverted.
key_insight: The prank showed him that attention follows marketing and simplicity claims, even though real software needs complexity and honest design.
